LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard binary package from a GitHub release. The `source` array fetches a prebuilt `.pkg.tar.zst` archive over HTTPS from the project's official GitHub repository, which is expected behavior. The `sha256sums` array provides a fixed checksum for the archive, ensuring integrity. There is no Git clone with mutable ref, no `pkgver()` function that executes commands, and no top-level command substitution or code execution during sourcing. The only executed logic during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` and `makepkg --nobuild` is parsing the static variables and downloading the source — both safe operations in this context. While the license is proprietary and the binary nature limits deeper inspection, no evidence of malicious behavior is present in the scope of this narrow gate.
</details>
<summary>
Safe; standard binary package from GitHub
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a pre-built binary package from the official GitHub release page of the project (https://github.com/Happ-proxy/happ-desktop/releases), which is consistent with the package's stated purpose as a "bin" (precompiled) package. The source URL points directly to a versioned release asset, and the checksum (`sha256sums`) is explicitly defined, ensuring integrity. The `package()` function extracts the contents of the binary tarball into the destination directory while excluding metadata files (`.PKGINFO`, `.MTREE`, `.INSTALL`), a common practice when repackaging existing packages.

There are no suspicious operations such as dynamic downloads at runtime, use of `eval`, `base64` decoding, or execution of fetched scripts. Network access is limited to the declared upstream over HTTPS, and the operations performed are confined to unpacking and installing files into the package directory. The package also includes a `.install` script for privilege elevation via polkit, which is noted as an optional dependency and falls within expected behavior for a TUN/VPN application needing root privileges.

While the package skips stripping binaries (`!strip`) and installs a precompiled binary (increasing trust assumptions), these are typical for `-bin` packages in AUR and do not themselves indicate malicious intent. The overall behavior aligns with standard packaging practices for distributing closed-source or prebuilt software on Arch Linux.
</details>
<summary>
Standard prebuilt package; no malicious behavior detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing happ-desktop-bin.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard prebuilt package; no malicious behavior detected
LLM auditresponse for happ-desktop-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `happ-desktop-bin.install` script performs standard post-installation and removal tasks typical of AUR packages. It updates desktop, MIME, and icon databases via `update-desktop-database`, `update-mime-database`, and `gtk-update-icon-cache`, which is common practice for GUI applications. It ensures bundled binaries have executable permissions, which is expected for a binary package that includes precompiled tools like `xray`, `sing-box`, and `tun2proxy-bin`. The script enables and starts the `happd.service` daemon during installation and stops/disables it on removal, which is appropriate for a system-level service requiring root privileges (e.g. for TUN/VPN access). These operations are transparent, use hardcoded paths, and do not involve downloading or executing external code.

The script does not contain obfuscated commands, network calls, or data exfiltration attempts. All actions are confined to the package's own files and standard system integration points. It removes only expected log and runtime-generated directories on uninstall. The use of `systemctl` is limited to the declared service, and fallbacks (`|| true`) prevent failures from breaking the package manager. While the daemon runs as root and the package installs multiple network tools (indicating elevated privileges), this aligns with the functionality of a desktop application managing network routing and filtering. These are upstream design choices, not supply-chain compromises.

No malicious or suspicious behavior was found. The script follows best practices for AUR install hooks and performs only expected system integration steps.
</details>
<summary>
Standard install script; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

The PKGBUILD contains no top-level or pkgver() code that would execute when sourced by makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare or makepkg --printsrcinfo. All variable assignments are static; there are no command substitutions, eval, or other dangerous constructs. The source array references a direct HTTPS download from the project's own GitHub releases, which is a standard and expected source. The install file is not sourced during these commands (only during the package install phase). Therefore, no malicious code can run during the requested operations.
No dangerous code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing. + Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.[/] Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent... makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent... makepkg --printsrcinfo successful Reviewing required files... Status: SKIPPED -- Skipping binary file: happ-desktop-bin-2.18.1.pkg.tar.zst LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD: SAFE
The PKGBUILD is a standard binary package for the AUR. It downloads a pre-compiled `.pkg.tar.zst` archive from the project's official GitHub releases (`github.com/Happ-proxy/happ-desktop`). The SHA-256 checksum is pinned and provided, ensuring integrity. The `package()` function simply extracts the archive into the package directory, excluding metadata files (`.PKGINFO`, `.MTREE`, `.INSTALL`). No obfuscated code, suspicious network requests, dangerous commands, or file operations outside of normal packaging procedures are present. The `install` script is referenced but not provided; even if it contains typical post-install actions, that would be normal. The file complies with standard AUR packaging practices and shows no signs of supply-chain manipulation.
Standard binary PKGBUILD from upstream GitHub release. [2/3] Reviewing happ-desktop-bin.install... + Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary PKGBUILD from upstream GitHub release. LLM auditresponse for happ-desktop-bin.install: SAFE
This is a standard Arch Linux package install script for the "happ" application (a VPN/tunneling utility). It performs routine post-install/post-remove operations: - Updating MIME, desktop database, and icon caches (standard for any package providing desktop entries). - Making the application's own binaries under `/opt/happ/bin/` executable. - Enabling and starting a systemd service (`happd.service`) for the application. - Cleaning up the application's own runtime artifacts (`/opt/happ/bin/core/routing`, log files under `/var/log`). There is no evidence of malicious activity such as exfiltration, downloading or executing untrusted code, obfuscated commands, or tampering with unrelated system files. All operations target the package's own installation paths and standard system caches — consistent with the application's stated purpose as a VPN/tunneling daemon.
